title: Chemical Bonding
subject: Chemistry
text: Chemical Bonding Types of Bonds - Ionic - Covalent - Coordinate VSEPR Theory Predicts shape of molecules. Examples: CH4 → tetrahedral NH3 → trigonal pyramidal H2O → bent Hybridization sp → linear sp² → trigonal planar sp³ → tetrahedral

title: Chemical Equilibrium
subject: Chemistry
text: Chemical Equilibrium Law of Mass Action For reaction: aA + bB ⇌ cC + dD K = [C]^c[D]^d / [A]^a[B]^b Types of Equilibrium 1. Homogeneous 2. Heterogeneous Le Chatelier Principle System shifts to counteract external change. Factors: - Concentration - Temperature - Pressure Equilibrium Constant Kc → concentration Kp → pressure Relation: Kp = Kc(RT)^(Δn)

title: Python Basics
subject: Computer Science
text: Python Basics Variables x = 10 name = "Alice" Data Types - int - float - string - list - dictionary Conditional Statements if x > 0: print("Positive") Loops for i in range(5): print(i)

title: English Tenses
subject: English
text: English Tenses Present Tense Simple Present Example: She writes a letter. Past Tense Simple Past She wrote a letter. Future Tense She will write a letter. Important Rules Use present tense for universal truths. Example: The sun rises in the east.

title: Derivatives
subject: Maths
text: Derivatives Derivative represents rate of change. dy/dx = lim h→0 (f(x+h) − f(x))/h Standard Derivatives d/dx (xⁿ) = nxⁿ⁻¹ d/dx (sin x) = cos x d/dx (cos x) = −sin x d/dx (tan x) = sec²x Product Rule d(uv)/dx = u dv/dx + v du/dx Chain Rule dy/dx = dy/du × du/dx Applications - Slope of tangent - Maxima & minima - Velocity & acceleration -

title: Limits
subject: Maths
text: Limits Definition Limit describes behavior of a function as input approaches a value. lim x→a f(x) Standard Limits lim x→0 (sin x / x) = 1 lim x→0 (1 − cos x)/x² = ½ lim x→0 (e^x −1)/x = 1 Important Techniques 1. Factorization 2. Rationalization 3. Standard limits 4. L'Hospital rule L'Hospital Rule If limit gives 0/0 or ∞/∞: lim f/g = lim f'/g'

title: Newton's Laws of Motion
subject: Physics
text: Newton's Laws of Motion First Law (Law of Inertia) An object remains at rest or continues to move with constant velocity unless acted upon by a net external force. Types of inertia: - Inertia of rest - Inertia of motion - Inertia of direction Second Law Force equals rate of change of momentum. F = dp/dt For constant mass: F = ma Third Law For every action there is an equal and opposite reaction. Important properties: - Forces act on different bodies - They are equal in magnitude - They act simultaneously Free Body Diagram (FBD) Steps: 1. Isolate object 2. Draw all forces acting 3. Resolve forces 4. Apply Newton's second law Important JEE Concepts Pseudo force in non-inertial frame: F = ma − ma₀ where a₀ = acceleration of frame. Typical JEE Problems - Block on inclined plane - Connected pulley systems - Elevator problems

title: Work Energy Power
subject: Physics
text: Work Energy Power Work W = F·s·cosθ Unit: Joule Kinetic Energy K = ½mv² Work Energy Theorem Work done by net force equals change in kinetic energy. W = ΔK Potential Energy Gravitational: U = mgh Elastic: U = ½kx² Power P = dW/dt Average Power: P = W/t Instantaneous Power: P = F·v Conservative Forces - Work independent of path - Potential energy defined Examples: - Gravitational - Spring force Mechanical Energy E = K + U Conserved if only conservative forces act.
